title: Layered Verification Architecture: Phase 1 & 2 Foundation (HLX-504 to HLX-509)
description: This tour walks through the implementation of the Layered Verification Architecture (the Sandwich Model) across six tickets (HLX-504 through HLX-509). The motivation is clear from production data: 11.6% of completed runs end in a terminal UNVERIFIED state, wasting agent work. The changes introduce new Prisma enum values (NEEDS_CREDENTIALS, IMPOSSIBLE_SPEC), TypeScript types for verification cascades, new workflow step catalog entries, new run-store atomic functions, and prompt-level changes to product/tech-research/implementation steps — all working together to give the pipeline richer, structured verification outcomes instead of a single binary gate.

steps.description: HLX-504 (Ticket 1A) is the root of the dependency chain. Production metrics show 139 of 1,200 completed runs (11.6%) end in a terminal UNVERIFIED state — work that is neither merged nor actionably failed. The core problem is that verification is a monolithic gate conflating QA, demo capture, and scenario acceptance. This ticket initiates the fix by extending the Prisma schema with two new terminal statuses: NEEDS_CREDENTIALS (the agent hit an auth wall it cannot cross) and IMPOSSIBLE_SPEC (the spec is internally contradictory or platform-impossible). It also adds a demoContent Json? column to SandboxRun so demo artifacts can be stored alongside the run record. Everything downstream (TypeScript types, run-store functions, workflow prompts) depends on these Prisma changes being applied and the client regenerated first.
steps.title: Overview: Why This Change Exists

steps.description: With the Prisma client regenerated by 1A, this ticket extends the TypeScript type layer in src/helix-workflow/step-executor/types.ts. The HelixWorkflowStepStatus union gains 'NEEDS_CREDENTIALS' and 'IMPOSSIBLE_SPEC' so the step executor can surface these outcomes without casting to unknown. Four new structured types are added: CascadeLayerResult (result of one verification layer), VerificationCascade (ordered collection of layer results), NeedsCredentialsJustification (structured explanation of the auth barrier), and SpecDeviations (itemised list of spec gaps). HelixWorkflowStepResult receives optional fields for all four, keeping the change non-breaking for existing consumers. Data flows upward: step executor populates these fields → orchestrator reads them → run-store persists them as JSON columns.
steps.title: HLX-505 (1B): TypeScript Type Foundation

steps.description: This ticket wires the new types into two runtime modules. In src/helix-workflow/helix-workflow-step-catalog.ts, the 'demo' step ID is added to the HelixWorkflowStepId union and the optional nonBlocking field is introduced on HelixWorkflowStep. The demo step entry { id: 'demo', title: 'Demo', nonBlocking: true, producesRepoArtifacts: false } is appended to HELIX_WORKFLOW_STEPS — nonBlocking: true means a demo failure does not abort the pipeline. In src/helix-workflow/orchestrator/run-store.ts, two new atomic functions are added following the existing markRunUnverified pattern (lines 364-389): markRunNeedsCredentials persists the NeedsCredentialsJustification into verificationReport JSON and sets the run status to NEEDS_CREDENTIALS; markRunImpossibleSpec does the same for SpecDeviations and IMPOSSIBLE_SPEC. Both functions reference the Prisma enum values introduced in 1A.
steps.title: HLX-506 (1C): Step Catalog + Run Store

steps.description: Phase 2 shifts from infrastructure to criteria production. This is a prompt-only change to sandbox-runtime-assets/workflow-steps/product/step-config.mjs. A 'User Scenarios' output section is added alongside the existing 'Success Criteria' block (around line 79). Each scenario uses the SCN-XX format with five mandatory fields: ID, Title, Precondition, Action, and Expected Outcome. The embedded guidelines specify 5-15 scenarios per ticket, describe WHAT not HOW, and must be platform-agnostic (web, NetSuite, future surfaces). These scenarios become the acceptance criteria that the verification cascade checks downstream — if an agent cannot satisfy a scenario, it documents a SpecDeviation referencing the SCN-XX ID.
steps.title: HLX-507 (2A): Product Step — SCN-XX User Scenarios

steps.description: A parallel prompt-only change to sandbox-runtime-assets/workflow-steps/tech-research/step-config.mjs. A 'Technical Checks' section is inserted alongside the existing 'Architecture Decisions' output (around lines 66-77). Each check uses the TCK-XX format with five mandatory fields: ID, Title, Decision Reference (which architecture decision and its verbatim text), Verification Method (code-inspection | behavioral), and Expected Evidence. Guidelines specify 3-8 checks per ticket and make clear that not every architecture decision needs a corresponding check — only those with non-obvious correctness criteria. TCK-XX items feed into the verification cascade as the code-level acceptance layer, complementing the scenario-level SCN-XX items from 2A.
steps.title: HLX-508 (2B): Tech-Research Step — TCK-XX Technical Checks

steps.description: The final Phase 2 ticket closes the loop on the implementation step prompt in sandbox-runtime-assets/workflow-steps/implementation/step-config.mjs. Two changes: (1) Inline check enforcement — advisory wording at lines 174-185 is hardened so every CHK-XX must receive an explicit pass / fail / blocked outcome in implementation-actual.md; silent omissions are prohibited. This eliminates the silent-skip pattern that produced UNVERIFIED outcomes. (2) Spec Deviations section — when the agent cannot fully achieve a product scenario (SCN-XX) or tech-research decision (TCK-XX) after best-effort attempt, it must document each deviation with a reference to the original item and a description of what was achieved instead. These deviations are what get stored in the SpecDeviations type (1B) and persisted by markRunImpossibleSpec (1C), completing the data flow from prompt → type → run-store → database.
steps.title: HLX-509 (2C): Implementation Step — Enforced Inline Checks + specDeviation

steps.description: The full pipeline remains coherent after these six tickets. The dependency order is strictly respected: 1A (Prisma schema + migration) → 1B (TypeScript types) → 1C (step catalog + run store) → 2A/2B/2C (prompt changes, no TypeScript deps). At runtime, the product step emits SCN-XX scenarios and the tech-research step emits TCK-XX checks; the implementation step must record explicit outcomes for every check and document any spec deviations. If the verification cascade detects an auth barrier, markRunNeedsCredentials is called atomically; if deviations make the spec unachievable, markRunImpossibleSpec is called. Both paths produce structured, queryable outcomes instead of terminal UNVERIFIED records, directly addressing the 11.6% waste rate identified in production. The build gates (npm run typecheck, npm run build, prisma generate) all pass because the changes are additive and non-breaking.
steps.title: Summary: End-to-End Data Flow Remains Clean
